refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated target rather than a fixed value.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under simulated and live network conditions (WiFi, cellular, hybrid) matching customer deployment scenarios. Measure end-to-end loss across the teleoperation link (operator console → vehicle → operator console). Acceptance: measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit for ≥95% of test runs.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command execution.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall monitor and report real-time network quality metrics (packet loss, latency, jitter) to the operator.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall adapt communication protocol parameters (compression, retransmission, redundancy) based on detected network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement adaptive packet prioritization and selective retransmission in the teleoperation protocol stack, with fallback to reduced-fidelity telemetry if packet loss exceeds threshold.
DP-B: Deploy redundant communication channels (dual-path routing, multi-carrier aggregation) with automatic failover to maintain packet loss below threshold.
DP-C: Implement forward error correction (FEC) and packet aggregation at the application layer, combined with network quality monitoring and operator alerts.
DP-D: Use a hybrid approach combining adaptive protocol tuning (DP-A), selective redundancy (DP-B), and application-layer FEC (DP-C) with real-time network diagnostics.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a multi-layered teleoperation communication architecture combining (1) adaptive protocol tuning with selective retransmission, (2) optional redundant channel activation on demand, and (3) application-layer forward error correction with real-time network quality monitoring and operator feedback.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation protocol design (Teleoperation module) with network infrastructure assumptions (Network Link interface). Mitigation: define clear SLAs for Network Link; design protocol to be agnostic to underlying carrier.

DP-D couples operator interface design (console feedback) with network diagnostics. Mitigation: standardize network quality metrics display; ensure diagnostics do not overload operator cognitive load.

DP-D couples vehicle-side communication stack with safety-critical command validation. Mitigation: ensure command validation is independent of packet loss recovery; use checksums and sequence numbers.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is moderately coupled but manageable through clear interface contracts and layered architecture. The multi-layered approach reduces single-point-of-failure risk and aligns with safety criticality.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer will define acceptable packet loss threshold and network conditions during requirements negotiation.
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle and operator console hardware support adaptive protocol tuning and FEC computation.
RISK: FEC overhead may increase latency; must validate that latency + packet loss recovery time remains acceptable for safety-critical commands.
RISK: Redundant channels increase cost and power consumption; customer may not accept trade-off.
RISK: Network quality monitoring may introduce additional packet overhead; must be carefully tuned to avoid paradoxical increase in loss.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify (1) protocol adaptation decision logic (thresholds, timers), (2) FEC parameters (code rate, block size), (3) redundancy activation criteria, and (4) operator alert thresholds. Decompose into sub-DPs for each layer to enable independent verification.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication stack, including protocol adaptation, error recovery, FEC, network quality monitoring, and operator feedback mechanisms. Interfaces with Vehicle for command/telemetry exchange, with Safety Services for safety-critical command validation, and with Network Link for underlying transport.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) from operator console; Teleoperation module encodes, prioritizes, and transmits via Network Link.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, system status) to operator console; Teleoperation module receives from Vehicle, applies FEC/recovery, and delivers to console.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints from Safety Services (e.g., geofence, speed limits); Teleoperation module enforces constraints on incoming commands. Transmits safety-critical events (e.g., packet loss exceeding threshold, communication failure) to Safety Services for operator alert and emergency stop logic.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying transport layer (WiFi, cellular, hybrid); Teleoperation module sends encoded packets and receives acknowledgments; monitors link quality metrics (RSSI, latency, loss).

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of packet loss mitigation logic. All protocol adaptation, FEC, and network quality monitoring are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert interface requires coordination with Safety Services for critical thresholds.

Vehicle module should not own teleoperation protocol logic; it is a consumer of commands and producer of telemetry. However, Vehicle may need to implement command validation and acknowledgment logic, which couples to Teleoperation's retransmission strategy. High diffusion risk if packet loss recovery is split between Vehicle and Teleoperation.

Safety Services owns safety-critical decision logic (e.g., emergency stop on communication loss). However, Safety Services should not own the teleoperation protocol itself. Coordination required: Teleoperation must alert Safety Services when packet loss exceeds threshold; Safety Services must define acceptable thresholds. High diffusion risk if safety validation is tightly coupled to protocol adaptation.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container but should not own detailed teleoperation protocol logic. Containment score reflects that NGHE Solution may define overall communication architecture and SLAs, but implementation is delegated to Teleoperation module.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D (multi-layered communication architecture). It has clear ownership of protocol design, error recovery, and network quality monitoring. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimize diffusion. Safety Services coordination is necessary but manageable via SafetyAlert interface.
containment_risks: RISK: If Vehicle module implements its own command validation or acknowledgment logic, packet loss recovery may be duplicated or inconsistent. MITIGATION: Define clear ownership: Teleoperation owns protocol-level recovery; Vehicle owns command semantic validation.

RISK: If Safety Services defines packet loss thresholds independently, Teleoperation may not have visibility into safety-critical limits. MITIGATION: Establish SafetyAlert interface contract specifying threshold negotiation and real-time alerts.

RISK: Network Link interface may not provide sufficient visibility into link quality metrics (RSSI, latency, jitter). MITIGATION: Extend Network Link interface to expose quality metrics; Teleoperation consumes these for adaptive tuning.

RISK: Operator console (not explicitly modeled as a module) may not support real-time network diagnostics display. MITIGATION: Define Teleoperation output interface for network quality metrics; console implementation is out of scope but interface is contractual.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the boundary between Teleoperation and Vehicle: (1) Teleoperation owns protocol-level packet loss recovery, FEC, and network quality monitoring. (2) Vehicle owns command semantic validation and acknowledgment generation. (3) Define explicit SafetyAlert interface contract specifying packet loss threshold negotiation, real-time alerts, and emergency stop triggers. (4) Extend Network Link interface to expose link quality metrics (RSSI, latency, loss) for Teleoperation consumption.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (multi-layered communication architecture) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold). DP-D provides multiple mechanisms (adaptive protocol tuning, selective redundancy, FEC, network quality monitoring) to achieve the functional requirement. The coupling check identified manageable risks (protocol-network coupling, operator interface coupling, command validation coupling), all mitigable through clear interface contracts. DP-D is neither over-constrained nor under-specified; it provides sufficient guidance for implementation while allowing flexibility in parameter tuning.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK: If Vehicle module implements command acknowledgment logic, retransmission strategy may be duplicated. MITIGATION: Teleoperation owns retransmission; Vehicle provides acknowledgment only.

DIFFUSION RISK: If Safety Services defines packet loss thresholds without Teleoperation input, safety-critical limits may be unrealistic. MITIGATION: SafetyAlert interface establishes threshold negotiation protocol.
DIFFUSION RISK: If Network Link does not expose quality metrics, Teleoperation cannot adapt protocol. MITIGATION: Extend Network Link interface specification.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 95% containment score for DP-D. The module owns protocol design, error recovery, and network quality monitoring. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimize diffusion. Ownership is clear; no competing modules claim primary responsibility. Recommended revisions focus on clarifying boundaries with Vehicle and Safety Services, not on reassigning ownership.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: FR-NGHE-009 is verifiable via packet loss measurement under simulated and live network conditions. Teleoperation module can be tested in isolation (with mock Vehicle and Network Link) or integrated with full system. Acceptance criteria (packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold) are measurable and objective. Sub-FRs (error detection/recovery, network quality monitoring, adaptive protocol tuning) are independently verifiable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of FR-NGHE-009 to Teleoperation module is clear. Sub-FRs are allocated to Teleoperation sub-components (protocol stack, network quality monitor, adaptive tuning engine). Safety-critical aspects (emergency stop on communication loss) are allocated to Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface. Operator feedback (network quality display) is allocated to operator console (out of scope but interface is contractual).

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1: Decompose FR-NGHE-009 into sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1, 009.2, 009.3) and allocate each to specific Teleoperation sub-components. This enables independent verification and clearer ownership.

REVISION 2: Establish explicit SafetyAlert interface contract specifying (a) packet loss threshold negotiation, (b) real-time alert format and frequency, (c) emergency stop trigger conditions. This clarifies boundary between Teleoperation and Safety Services.
REVISION 3: Extend Network Link interface specification to expose link quality metrics (RSSI, latency, loss, jitter). This enables Teleoperation to implement adaptive protocol tuning.
REVISION 4: Define Teleoperation output interface for operator console (network quality metrics display). This clarifies what information is available to operator for situational awareness.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic; it encompasses multiple concerns (protocol adaptation, error recovery, network quality monitoring, safety-critical alerting). Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1, 009.2, 009.3) are more atomic and enable independent design and verification. DP-D is sound but requires decomposition into sub-DPs for each layer (adaptive protocol tuning, selective redundancy, FEC, network quality monitoring). Teleoperation module is the correct owner, but boundaries with Vehicle and Safety Services require clarification via interface contracts. Proceeding to next decomposition level will enable more detailed design, clearer ownership, and reduced integration risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall implement error detection and recovery mechanisms (retransmission, FEC, packet aggregation) to minimize packet loss impact on command execution.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall monitor and report real-time network quality metrics (packet loss, latency, jitter) to the operator and safety services.

FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall adapt communication protocol parameters (compression, retransmission strategy, FEC code rate) based on detected network conditions to maintain packet loss below threshold.

assumptions: Customer will define acceptable packet loss threshold (e.g., ≤1%) and network conditions (WiFi, cellular, hybrid) during requirements negotiation.
Vehicle and operator console hardware support adaptive protocol tuning and FEC computation without unacceptable latency increase.
Network Link interface can expose link quality metrics (RSSI, latency, loss) for Teleoperation consumption.
Safety Services will define packet loss thresholds and emergency stop triggers via SafetyAlert interface.
Operator console can display real-time network quality metrics without overwhelming operator cognitive load.
open_risks: RISK: FEC overhead may increase latency; must validate that latency + packet loss recovery time remains acceptable for safety-critical commands (e.g., emergency stop).
RISK: Redundant channels increase cost and power consumption; customer may not accept trade-off. Requires cost-benefit analysis.
RISK: Network quality monitoring may introduce additional packet overhead; must be carefully tuned to avoid paradoxical increase in loss.
RISK: Operator console may not support real-time network diagnostics display; requires clarification of operator interface requirements.
RISK: Vehicle module may implement its own command validation or acknowledgment logic, leading to duplication or inconsistency with Teleoperation recovery strategy.
evidence_needed: Customer-defined packet loss threshold and network conditions (WiFi, cellular, hybrid deployment scenarios).
Hardware specifications for Vehicle and operator console (processing power, memory, latency budget for FEC/protocol adaptation).
Network Link interface specification (available quality metrics, update frequency, accuracy).
Safety Services requirements (packet loss thresholds, emergency stop triggers, alert format).
Operator interface requirements (network quality metrics display, alert presentation, cognitive load limits).
Latency budget analysis: end-to-end latency for safety-critical commands (e.g., emergency stop) with FEC and recovery overhead.
Cost-benefit analysis for redundant channels (dual-path routing, multi-carrier aggregation).
